description: PawCheck toxic foods and substances quick reference. CRITICAL safety content - poisoning is one of the highest-stakes scenarios because incorrect or delayed advice can kill a pet within hours. All entries include dose thresholds where known, but ANY suspected poisoning is an emergency regardless of dose - always recommend immediate vet contact.

animal_poison_line.cost: From £35 per call (cheaper than out-of-hours vet)
animal_poison_line.note: First port of call for suspected poisoning - triages whether vet visit is needed

vets_now.note: UK out-of-hours emergency vet network - find nearest clinic

if_recent_ingestion: Phone the Animal Poison Line (01202 509000) or your vet IMMEDIATELY - do not wait for symptoms
Have ready: pet's weight, what was eaten, how much, when, and the product packaging if possible
Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically told to by a vet - some substances cause more damage coming back up
Do NOT give milk, salt water, or any home remedies - these are myths and can make things worse
Keep the pet calm and warm during transport
Bring any packaging, leaves, or vomit sample to the vet
if_symptoms_already_present: EMERGENCY - go straight to the nearest open vet, phone ahead
Note all symptoms and timing for the vet
If seizing, keep the pet on the floor away from furniture - do not restrain

dog_toxins.name: Chocolate
dog_toxins.why_toxic: Dogs metabolise theobromine much more slowly than humans, allowing it to accumulate to toxic levels. Affects heart, central nervous system, and kidneys.

danger_by_type.risk: Very low - fat content is the bigger issue
Moderate - problem dose around 200g for a 10kg dog
High - problem dose around 50g for a 10kg dog

dog_toxins.rough_toxic_dose: Mild signs: ~20mg/kg theobromine. Severe: ~40-50mg/kg. Potentially fatal: ~100-200mg/kg.
example_calculations: 10kg Labrador eats one square (25g) of dark chocolate (~125mg theobromine) = 12.5mg/kg = monitor closely
5kg terrier eats half a milk chocolate bar (50g, ~85mg theobromine) = 17mg/kg = vet call
20kg dog eats a 100g bar of dark chocolate = 25mg/kg = vet visit, likely treatment needed
symptoms: Rapid heart rate or irregular rhythm
what_to_do: Phone Animal Poison Line or vet IMMEDIATELY with: dog's weight, type of chocolate, amount eaten, time eaten
Keep the wrapper / packaging to show the cocoa percentage
Do NOT induce vomiting unless instructed
Treatment usually involves induced vomiting (if recent), activated charcoal, IV fluids, and monitoring
dog_toxins.prognosis: Good with prompt treatment. Most dogs recover fully if seen within a few hours of ingestion.

dog_toxins.name: Grapes, raisins, sultanas, currants
dog_toxins.severity: VERY HIGH - any amount potentially fatal
dog_toxins.why_toxic: Cause acute kidney failure in dogs. The toxic mechanism is still not fully understood and the toxic dose varies enormously between individuals - some dogs eat handfuls without harm, others die from a single grape. ALWAYS treat as an emergency.

danger_by_type.risk: Higher concentration - even smaller amounts dangerous

danger_by_type.type: Christmas pudding / mince pies / hot cross buns
danger_by_type.risk: Common festive poisoning - all contain dried vine fruit

dog_toxins.rough_toxic_dose: No safe dose established. Cases of kidney failure reported from as little as 4-5 grapes in a small dog.
dog_toxins.symptoms_onset: Vomiting within 6-12 hours. Kidney failure signs within 24-72 hours.
what_to_do: EMERGENCY - phone vet immediately, regardless of amount eaten
Treatment is most effective if started within 2 hours of ingestion (induce vomiting, activated charcoal)
After symptoms develop, prognosis is much poorer
Hospitalisation with IV fluids for 48-72 hours is standard treatment
dog_toxins.prognosis: Excellent if treated within 2 hours. Once kidney failure develops, prognosis becomes guarded to poor.

dog_toxins.name: Xylitol (artificial sweetener)
dog_toxins.severity: VERY HIGH - extremely dangerous in tiny amounts
dog_toxins.why_toxic: Triggers massive insulin release in dogs causing sudden, severe blood sugar crash (hypoglycaemia). At higher doses also causes acute liver failure. Found in many products owners don't expect.
common_sources: Sugar-free chewing gum (especially Wrigley's Extra, Orbit, Trident)
Sugar-free peanut butter (CHECK LABELS - some brands now use xylitol)
Sugar-free baked goods and ice cream
dog_toxins.rough_toxic_dose: Hypoglycaemia: 0.1g/kg (a single piece of gum may contain 0.3-1g xylitol - enough to poison a small dog). Liver failure: 0.5g/kg.
example_calculations: 10kg dog eats one piece of gum containing 1g xylitol = 0.1g/kg = hypoglycaemia risk - EMERGENCY
5kg dog eats two pieces of gum = potential liver failure dose
dog_toxins.symptoms_onset: Hypoglycaemia within 30 minutes to 12 hours. Liver damage signs 12-72 hours later.
symptoms: Yellowing of gums or eyes (liver failure - later sign)
what_to_do: EMERGENCY - phone vet IMMEDIATELY, do not wait for symptoms
Treatment must start within an hour for best outcomes
If the dog is conscious and at home, you can rub a small amount of honey or golden syrup on the gums while travelling to the vet (does not replace vet care)
Hospitalisation with IV dextrose, liver protectants, and monitoring for 24-72 hours
dog_toxins.prognosis: Good for hypoglycaemia alone if treated quickly. Guarded if liver failure develops.

dog_toxins.name: Onions, garlic, leeks, chives, shallots (allium family)
dog_toxins.why_toxic: Damage red blood cells causing anaemia. All forms toxic - raw, cooked, dried, powdered. Powdered/concentrated forms (gravy granules, onion powder, soup mix) are most dangerous because the toxin is concentrated.

danger_by_type.risk: Moderate - cooking does not destroy the toxin
Lower but still toxic if eaten in quantity

dog_toxins.rough_toxic_dose: Onion: 15-30g per kg body weight (a 10kg dog needs about 150-300g of onion). Garlic is roughly 5x more toxic than onion. Repeated small doses can accumulate.
common_accidental_exposures: Leftover takeaway curry, Chinese food, pasta sauce
dog_toxins.symptoms_onset: 1-5 days (delayed) - this is why it's often missed
symptoms: Dark / red-tinged urine (red blood cell breakdown)
what_to_do: Phone vet or Animal Poison Line - even small amounts of concentrated forms warrant advice
Bring the packaging if relevant
Treatment may include induced vomiting (if recent), activated charcoal, and blood tests
Severe cases may need blood transfusion
dog_toxins.prognosis: Good with prompt treatment. Most dogs recover fully.

dog_toxins.name: Macadamia nuts
dog_toxins.why_toxic: Specific to macadamias - other nuts do not cause the same effect. Causes weakness in the back legs and a generalised illness.

danger_by_type.risk: Moderate - watch for added chocolate too

dog_toxins.rough_toxic_dose: Around 2g per kg (a 10kg dog eating about 20g of nuts can show signs)
symptoms: Weakness in the back legs (classic sign)
Inability to stand or walk normally
what_to_do: Phone vet or Animal Poison Line
If recent ingestion, induced vomiting may be recommended
Symptoms usually resolve within 24-48 hours with supportive care
Check whether any chocolate was attached (changes urgency significantly)
dog_toxins.prognosis: Excellent - full recovery is typical within 1-2 days.

dog_toxins.name: Alcohol
dog_toxins.why_toxic: Dogs are much smaller and more sensitive to ethanol than humans. Causes central nervous system depression, breathing difficulty, low blood sugar, and dangerous drops in body temperature.
common_sources: Beer, wine, spirits left in glasses
Christmas mulled wine and sherry trifle
Bread dough (yeast produces ethanol as it rises in the warm stomach)
dog_toxins.rough_toxic_dose: Around 5-8ml of pure alcohol per kg can be fatal. A 10kg dog drinking half a glass of wine is at significant risk.
what_to_do: EMERGENCY - phone vet immediately
Keep the dog warm during transport (hypothermia is a real risk)
Do NOT induce vomiting - aspiration risk is too high
Treatment: IV fluids, warming, breathing support, blood sugar monitoring
dog_toxins.prognosis: Good for mild cases. Severe cases (deep coma, seizures) have guarded prognosis.

dog_toxins.name: Caffeine
dog_toxins.why_toxic: Similar mechanism to chocolate but acts faster. Affects heart and nervous system.
common_sources: Coffee grounds (especially used grounds in compost / bin)
dog_toxins.rough_toxic_dose: Around 20mg/kg causes signs, 100-200mg/kg potentially fatal. An espresso has 60-100mg.
symptoms: Rapid heart rate or irregular rhythm
what_to_do: Phone vet or Animal Poison Line
Bring the source (cup, packet, can) for identification
Treatment: induced vomiting if recent, activated charcoal, IV fluids, heart monitoring

dog_toxins.name: Bread dough (raw yeast dough)
dog_toxins.active_ingredient: Yeast fermentation produces ethanol and CO2 in the warm stomach
dog_toxins.why_toxic: Two problems: dough expands rapidly in the stomach causing painful bloating that can lead to gastric dilatation-volvulus (GDV), and yeast fermentation produces ethanol causing alcohol poisoning.
common_sources: Homemade bread / pizza dough left to rise
dog_toxins.rough_toxic_dose: Any amount can be dangerous in small dogs. Even small amounts in larger dogs may cause symptoms.
symptoms: Unproductive retching (trying to vomit but nothing comes up)
what_to_do: EMERGENCY - phone vet immediately
Do NOT induce vomiting at home - dough mass can block the throat
Treatment: gastric lavage or surgery to remove the dough, plus alcohol toxicity treatment
dog_toxins.prognosis: Good with prompt vet treatment. Delayed cases can develop GDV which is life-threatening.

dog_toxins.name: Cooked bones
dog_toxins.severity: MEDIUM-HIGH - mechanical injury rather than toxic
dog_toxins.why_dangerous: Cooked bones (especially chicken, turkey, pork chop, fish) splinter into sharp shards that can perforate the gut, get stuck in the throat, or cause severe constipation. Raw bones are safer (though still risky) - cooked bones are particularly dangerous.
symptoms: Choking or gagging immediately after eating
Drooling or pawing at the mouth
what_to_do: If choking - emergency vet immediately
If swallowed without choking - phone vet for advice (X-rays may be needed)
Do NOT induce vomiting - bone fragments can re-damage the throat
Monitor stools for several days - blood or no passing of stool requires vet attention
dog_toxins.prognosis: Most dogs pass small bone fragments without issue. Perforation or obstruction requires surgery.

dog_toxins.name: Cannabis / THC
dog_toxins.why_toxic: Dogs are much more sensitive than humans. Most exposures are accidental - eating an owner's edibles or finding discarded joints.
common_sources: Cannabis edibles (often contain chocolate too - double toxicity)
symptoms: Dribbling urine while standing (very characteristic sign)
what_to_do: Phone vet - be honest about the exposure (no legal consequences for telling the vet, and honesty speeds treatment)
Keep the dog warm and in a calm environment
Treatment is supportive: IV fluids, anti-nausea medication, monitoring
dog_toxins.prognosis: Generally good. Most dogs recover within 24-72 hours. Edibles with chocolate are higher risk.

dog_toxins.name: Mouldy food / blue cheese
dog_toxins.why_toxic: Mouldy food and blue cheeses contain mycotoxins that cause severe tremors and seizures.
common_sources: Mouldy bread, cheese, or fruit from the bin
dog_toxins.rough_toxic_dose: Variable but blue cheese in any quantity warrants advice. Compost ingestion is often serious.
what_to_do: EMERGENCY - phone vet immediately if tremors present
Keep cool and calm
Treatment: induced vomiting (if recent), activated charcoal, muscle relaxants, IV fluids

dog_toxins.name: Common toxic plants (dog)

highest_risk_plants_uk.risk: VERY HIGH - sudden death possible from cardiac toxin
HIGH - especially bulbs, severe GI signs
MEDIUM - kidney damage if eaten in quantity
MEDIUM - GI obstruction more than toxicity
MEDIUM-HIGH - bulbs more toxic than leaves

what_to_do: Phone vet or Animal Poison Line - identify the plant if possible
Bring a leaf / flower / bulb sample to the vet
Treatment depends on the plant - some require very specific antidotes
Do not assume garden plants are safe - many UK garden plants are toxic
prevention: Check the RHS or Dogs Trust toxic plants list before planting
Supervise dogs around spring bulbs
Remove fallen yew berries, acorns, conkers from areas where dogs roam

cat_toxins.name: Lilies
cat_toxins.severity: EXTREMELY HIGH - cats only - rapid kidney failure
cat_toxins.why_toxic: ALL parts of true lilies (Lilium and Hemerocallis species) are highly toxic to cats. Pollen alone, brushed onto fur and then licked off during grooming, can cause fatal kidney failure. Cats are uniquely sensitive - dogs are not affected the same way.
less_toxic_lily_like_plants: Peace lily (Spathiphyllum) - causes mouth irritation but not kidney failure
Calla lily - similar to peace lily
Lily of the valley - DIFFERENT toxin (heart, not kidney) but also dangerous
exposure_routes: Eating any part of the plant
Pollen on fur licked off during grooming
cat_toxins.symptoms_onset: Vomiting within hours, kidney failure within 24-72 hours
symptoms: Vomiting (often the first sign, within 2-6 hours)
Yellow pollen visible on fur or in vomit (diagnostic clue)
Reduced or no urination (kidney shutdown - usually 24-72 hours after exposure)
what_to_do: EMERGENCY - phone vet IMMEDIATELY, do not wait for symptoms
Bring the plant or a photo for identification
Treatment must start within 18 hours of exposure for best outcomes
Standard treatment: induced vomiting (if recent), activated charcoal, aggressive IV fluids for 48-72 hours, kidney function monitoring
prevention: NEVER bring lilies into a home with cats - including bouquets
Warn florists - many bouquets contain lily species without obvious labels
Remove lilies from gardens cats can access
Wash any pollen off a cat's fur immediately if exposure suspected
cat_toxins.prognosis: Excellent if treated within 18 hours of ingestion. Once kidney failure is established, mortality is very high.

cat_toxins.name: Paracetamol (acetaminophen / Calpol / Tylenol)
cat_toxins.severity: EXTREMELY HIGH - cats lack the enzyme to process it - tiny doses are fatal
cat_toxins.why_toxic: Cats lack the liver enzyme (glucuronyl transferase) needed to safely metabolise paracetamol. Even a single regular-strength tablet can be fatal. Causes liver failure and damages red blood cells (methaemoglobinaemia).
common_accidental_exposures: Owner gives paracetamol thinking it's safe for pain (it is NOT)
Cat finds and chews tablets left out
Children's liquid paracetamol spilled and licked up
cat_toxins.rough_toxic_dose: As little as 10mg/kg can cause toxicity. A single 500mg adult tablet is enough to kill an average 4-5kg cat.
symptoms: Brown or chocolate-coloured gums (methaemoglobinaemia - very characteristic)
Swelling of the face and paws (very characteristic)
Yellowing of the eyes or gums (liver damage)
what_to_do: EMERGENCY - phone vet IMMEDIATELY, this is one of the most urgent poisonings in cats
Treatment includes induced vomiting (if very recent), activated charcoal, IV fluids, and specific antidotes (N-acetylcysteine)
NEVER give human painkillers to cats unless prescribed by a vet
cat_toxins.prognosis: Guarded even with treatment. Best outcomes when treated within 2 hours of ingestion.

cat_toxins.name: Permethrin (dog flea treatment used on cats)
cat_toxins.severity: EXTREMELY HIGH - common preventable killer of cats
cat_toxins.why_toxic: Cats cannot metabolise permethrin and concentrated spot-on dog flea treatments are extremely toxic. One of the most common preventable poisoning deaths in cats. Even cats living with treated dogs can be poisoned by close contact or grooming.
common_accidental_exposures: Owner applies a dog flea spot-on to their cat by mistake
Cat grooms a recently treated dog
Cat sleeps where a treated dog has been
Household insecticide sprays at high concentration
Plug-in mosquito repellents with high permethrin content
cat_toxins.symptoms_onset: Within a few hours of exposure
symptoms: Twitching or jerking, especially ears, face, tail
what_to_do: EMERGENCY - go straight to the vet, phone ahead
If applied to skin: wash the cat thoroughly with lukewarm water and washing-up liquid (Fairy Liquid) before transport to the vet - this removes much of the chemical
Keep the cat warm but not hot
Treatment: aggressive seizure control, IV fluids, cooling, and lipid emulsion therapy in severe cases
prevention: Only use cat-specific flea treatments on cats
Read every label carefully - permethrin products clearly state 'do not use on cats'
Keep cats away from treated dogs for at least 24-48 hours after spot-on application
Stick to vet-prescribed products
cat_toxins.prognosis: Variable - mild cases recover well with treatment, severe seizures can be fatal.

cat_toxins.name: Antifreeze (ethylene glycol)
cat_toxins.severity: EXTREMELY HIGH - sweet taste attracts cats - fatal without rapid treatment
cat_toxins.why_toxic: Sweet tasting so cats and dogs are attracted to it. Even a teaspoon can kill a cat. Metabolised into compounds that destroy the kidneys.
common_sources: Car radiator coolant spills on driveways
cat_toxins.rough_toxic_dose: As little as 1.5ml/kg is potentially fatal in cats - a teaspoon for a small cat.
cat_toxins.symptoms_onset: Phase 1: 30 mins - 12 hours. Phase 2 (kidney failure): 24-72 hours.
symptoms: Phase 1: appears drunk, wobbly, vomiting, excessive thirst, excessive urination
Phase 2: appearing better briefly (DECEPTIVE - poisoning is ongoing)
Phase 3: severe lethargy, vomiting, reduced urination, seizures, death
what_to_do: EMERGENCY - immediate vet treatment, this is time-critical
Treatment must start within 3 hours for best outcomes
Antidote (ethanol or fomepizole) plus aggressive IV fluids and kidney support
prevention: Clean up driveway and garage spills immediately
Use propylene glycol antifreeze (less toxic) where available
Keep cats indoors during winter if you cannot control neighbourhood spills
Check garden water features
cat_toxins.prognosis: Excellent if treated within 3 hours. Poor once kidney failure develops.

cat_toxins.name: Ibuprofen, aspirin, and other NSAIDs
cat_toxins.severity: VERY HIGH - cats far more sensitive than dogs
cat_toxins.why_toxic: Cats metabolise NSAIDs extremely slowly. A single ibuprofen tablet can cause stomach ulcers and kidney failure.
cat_toxins.rough_toxic_dose: Ibuprofen: as low as 50mg/kg causes signs. A single 200mg tablet in a 4kg cat exceeds this.
symptoms: Black tarry stools (digested blood from stomach ulcers)
what_to_do: EMERGENCY - phone vet immediately, even a single tablet warrants treatment
NEVER give human painkillers to cats
Treatment: induced vomiting (if recent), activated charcoal, IV fluids, stomach protectants, kidney support
cat_toxins.prognosis: Good with prompt treatment. Guarded if kidney failure or perforated ulcer develops.

cat_toxins.name: Essential oils
cat_toxins.active_ingredient: Various - phenols and monoterpenes particularly problematic
cat_toxins.why_toxic: Cats lack key liver enzymes (glucuronyl transferase) to safely metabolise many essential oil compounds. Both direct contact and inhalation can cause toxicity.
exposure_routes: Cat licking oil residue off surfaces or paws
Reed diffusers knocked over and spilled
cat_toxins.symptoms_onset: Variable - hours to days for skin absorption, minutes for ingestion
symptoms: Red or irritated skin where oil touched
what_to_do: Move the cat to fresh air immediately if diffuser exposure
Wash off any skin contact with lukewarm water and gentle pet shampoo (NOT human soap)
Phone vet or Animal Poison Line
Bring the oil bottle / packaging for identification
prevention: Avoid essential oil diffusers in homes with cats, or use only in rooms cats cannot access
Never apply essential oils directly to cats
Keep all bottles tightly closed and out of reach

cat_toxins.name: Onions, garlic, leeks, chives
cat_toxins.severity: HIGH - cats more sensitive than dogs
cat_toxins.why_toxic: Damage red blood cells causing anaemia. Cats are roughly 4x more sensitive than dogs. Powdered and cooked forms still toxic.
cat_toxins.rough_toxic_dose: As little as 5g/kg can cause toxicity. A 4kg cat eating 20g of onion (a single ring of fried onion) can be at risk.
common_exposures: Baby food or jarred meat with onion (a common accidental poisoning)
Garlic supplements wrongly given to cats
Garlic-flavoured cat treats from unreliable sources
what_to_do: Phone vet or Animal Poison Line
Bring packaging if relevant
Treatment: induced vomiting (if recent), supportive care, possibly blood transfusion in severe cases

cat_toxins.name: Chocolate (cats)
cat_toxins.severity: HIGH but cats less likely to eat it than dogs
cat_toxins.why_toxic: Same mechanism as in dogs - cats cannot metabolise theobromine quickly. Cats rarely seek out chocolate (they cannot taste sweetness) but accidental exposures still occur with chocolate ice cream, milk drinks, or owners feeding chocolate.
cat_toxins.rough_toxic_dose: Similar to dogs: ~20mg/kg theobromine causes signs. A 4kg cat can be poisoned by 10-15g of dark chocolate.
what_to_do: Phone vet or Animal Poison Line with: cat's weight, type of chocolate, amount eaten
Bring the packaging
Treatment as for dogs - induced vomiting, activated charcoal, supportive care

cat_toxins.name: Raisins, grapes, sultanas (cats)
cat_toxins.severity: HIGH - same kidney risk as in dogs
cat_toxins.why_toxic: Less commonly eaten by cats but still potentially cause acute kidney failure. As in dogs, no safe dose is established.
common_exposures: Cake or biscuits with raisins or sultanas
cat_toxins.symptoms_onset: Vomiting within 6-12 hours, kidney signs 24-72 hours
what_to_do: EMERGENCY - phone vet regardless of amount
Treatment most effective within 2 hours: induced vomiting, activated charcoal, IV fluids for 48-72 hours

cat_toxins.name: Xylitol (cats - emerging risk)
cat_toxins.severity: Uncertain - traditionally thought safer for cats than dogs, but newer evidence suggests caution
cat_toxins.why_toxic: Historically thought to be only a problem in dogs. Recent veterinary opinion suggests cats may also be affected at high doses, though they rarely eat sweet foods. Always treat any sugar-free product exposure as potentially toxic.
what_to_do: Phone vet or Animal Poison Line for current guidance
Bring packaging
Better to assume toxicity than dismiss

cat_toxins.name: Raw fish (regular consumption)
cat_toxins.severity: MEDIUM - chronic concern rather than acute
cat_toxins.why_toxic: Some raw fish contain an enzyme that destroys thiamine (vitamin B1) in the cat's body. Long-term consumption causes thiamine deficiency leading to neurological problems. Single small exposures are not dangerous.
fish_with_thiaminase: Raw tuna (though most concerning is regular feeding)
symptoms_of_thiamine_deficiency: Head tilt or loss of balance
what_to_do: Switch to cooked fish (heat destroys the enzyme) or commercial cat food
If neurological signs present, urgent vet visit - thiamine supplementation reverses signs quickly if caught early
Tuna in particular should be a rare treat, not a staple

cat_toxins.name: Other common toxic plants (cats)

highest_risk_plants.risk: EXTREMELY HIGH - kidney failure (see separate entry)
LOW - mainly oral irritation, often over-stated

symptoms: Pawing at the mouth (oral irritation plants)
what_to_do: Phone vet or Animal Poison Line with the plant identified
Photo and a leaf sample help with identification
Treatment is plant-specific
prevention: Check the International Cat Care safe plants list before bringing greenery into the home
Never have lilies in a cat household
Be especially careful with bouquets - check ingredients with the florist
